Paracetamol.note: Reduce to 500mg per dose in elderly. Max 2g/day total.

Metformin.note: Kidney function declines with age. Reduce dose and monitor eGFR.

Aspirin.note: Low-dose Aspirin only in elderly. Higher doses dramatically increase GI bleed risk.

Atenolol.note: Elderly patients metabolize beta-blockers more slowly.

Metoprolol.note: Start low, go slow in elderly.

Ramipril.note: Elderly are prone to first-dose hypotension. Start at 1.25mg.

Enalapril.note: Reduce dose in elderly due to reduced renal clearance.

Amlodipine.note: Elderly have higher sensitivity to CCBs, causing more ankle swelling.

Furosemide.note: Elderly are at high risk of dehydration and electrolyte imbalances from loop diuretics.

Alprazolam.note: Benzodiazepines cause paradoxical agitation and falls in elderly. Use at minimum dose.

Clonazepam.note: Extremely cautious use in elderly. High fall risk.

Digoxin.note: Elderly have smaller volume of distribution. Even 0.25mg causes toxicity.

Prednisolone.note: Elderly are extremely susceptible to steroid side effects — osteoporosis, hyperglycemia, infections.

Glibenclamide.note: Long-acting — very high hypoglycemia risk in elderly. Prefer Gliclazide instead.

Ibuprofen.note: NSAIDs are high-risk in elderly. Use for shortest duration at lowest dose only.

Colchicine.note: Elderly have reduced renal clearance. Toxicity risk is high at normal adult doses.

Allopurinol.note: Start at 50-100mg in elderly. Titrate slowly based on uric acid response.

Amiodarone.note: Long half-life drug. Elderly need lower maintenance dose. Monitor thyroid and lung function.

Ciprofloxacin.note: Reduce dose in elderly due to lower renal clearance.

Losartan.note: Start low to avoid first-dose hypotension and dizziness in elderly.

Spironolactone.note: Elderly are at high risk of dangerous hyperkalemia. Start at 12.5mg.
